tree_title: Contact Lenses: Types and How They Work
overall_topic: Contact Lenses: Types and How They Work
questions: When researching contact lenses and overall eye health, you come across a major U.S. academic medical center that emphasizes getting an annual eye exam to detect vision problems early and maintain long-term eye health. Based on that organization’s publicly available information, what are the key features of its virtual second opinion service (including how the process works, pricing for U.S. and international patients, insurance or Medicare considerations, and geographic availability limitations), and what does it state about the training and services provided by its eye care specialists for exams and vision correction?

If a patient who wears contact lenses is advised to get an annual eye exam at a major U.S.-based nonprofit academic medical center known for promoting early detection of vision problems, and that same institution also offers a paid virtual second opinion program, what are the key features, process steps, pricing (including U.S. and international costs), insurance and Medicare policies, state or country availability limitations, and refund conditions of that virtual second opinion service? Additionally, what does this institution state about the qualifications and scope of its eye care providers, and how does it organize information about its departments and services for patients seeking specialized care?

Within the context of guidance on contact lenses and overall eye health, identify the major nonprofit academic medical center that advises patients to schedule yearly comprehensive eye examinations as part of maintaining healthy vision. What specific long-term benefits does this institution state can result from getting an annual eye exam, particularly in relation to early detection and sustained eye health?

An individual researching contact lenses wants to schedule an annual eye exam at a major nonprofit academic medical center in Ohio known for emphasizing preventive vision care. Beyond in-person eye services, this institution also provides (1) an online resource that allows users to explore all of its clinical departments, institutes, and services, and (2) a remote expert second-opinion program delivered in partnership with a global telehealth company. Identify this medical center and explain how its comprehensive online departments resource supports patients seeking eye care such as contact lens evaluations. Then, describe in detail how its remote second-opinion program works, including the registration and intake process, record collection and specialist matching, how recommendations are delivered, typical costs (U.S. and international), insurance considerations, and key state or country availability limitations.

A major U.S.-based nonprofit academic medical center that encourages patients to get annual eye exams for early detection of vision problems also provides extensive resources for people considering vision correction options such as contact lenses. Using publicly available information, explain three aspects of this organization’s patient services: (1) how patients can access a comprehensive overview of all its clinical departments, institutes and services; (2) how its remote expert review program works, including the main steps in the process, typical costs for domestic and international patients, and any notable geographic eligibility limitations; and (3) the level of training of its eye care specialists and the types of vision and eye care services they provide, particularly those relevant to exams and corrective lenses. Your response should synthesize information across these service areas.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Virtual Second Opinions Learn More Get Started (U.S.) Call: 216.444.3223 An easy and fast way to help you make a more informed, confident decision about your care. All from th
rounds.answer_a:
Cleveland Clinic—the academic medical center that urges everyone who wears contact lenses or otherwise “to have an eye examination every year to catch vision problems early and keep your eyes healthy” [2]—offers a nationwide Virtual Second Opinion (VSO) program with these features:  

• How it works: After enrolling online, a dedicated nurse collects your medical records and imaging. Within days you are matched to a Cleveland Clinic subspecialist who reviews the material, conducts a secure video visit, and sends a written report with recommendations; the whole process usually takes about two weeks [1].  

• Pricing: U.S. adult cases cost $1,850; pediatric cases $1,650. International patients pay $1,950. Optional add-ons are $50 for radiology rereads and $250 for pathology slide review; payment is made up-front by credit card, HSA/FSA or wire transfer [1].  

• Insurance/Medicare: The service bills no commercial insurance, Medicare or Medicaid; patients may try to obtain reimbursement on their own, but coverage is uncommon. Travel or follow-up care is not included [1].  

• Geographic availability: Because of state licensing limits, the VSO is not offered to residents of Alaska, Delaware, Louisiana, Mississippi, North Dakota, South Dakota or Wyoming, nor in U.S. territories [1].  

Eye-care training and services: Cleveland Clinic’s Cole Eye Institute staff includes board-certified ophthalmologists (medical school, 1-year internship, 3-year residency, many with additional fellowship training) and licensed optometrists/contact-lens specialists. They provide comprehensive and routine eye exams, contact-lens and eyeglass fitting, refractive surgery (LASIK/PRK), cataract surgery, low-vision rehabilitation and medical/surgical treatment of eye disease—all focused on preserving long-term vision health [2][3].  

## References  
[1] Cleveland Clinic. “Virtual Second Opinions.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/online-services/virtual-second-opinion  
[2] Cleveland Clinic Cole Eye Institute. “Contact Lens & Routine Eye Exams.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/departments/eye/contact-lens  
[3] Cleveland Clinic Cole Eye Institute. “About Us and Services.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/departments/eye
rounds.answer_b:
A major U.S. academic medical center matching that description is **Cleveland Clinic**. Its **Virtual Second Opinions** service lets patients submit medical records and imaging through a secure online platform; the case is then reviewed by a Cleveland Clinic specialist, who provides a written expert opinion and treatment recommendations, typically without the patient needing to travel for an in-person visit [1]. The published pricing is **$1,850 for U.S. patients** and **$2,100 for international patients** [1]. Cleveland Clinic states that **insurance is not accepted** for this service and that **Medicare does not cover** virtual second opinions, so patients pay out of pocket [1]. It also notes **geographic limitations**: the service is **not available in all U.S. states or all countries**, and availability depends on where the patient is located at the time of the request because of licensure and regulatory restrictions [1].

For eye care, Cleveland Clinic says annual eye exams help detect vision problems early and support long-term eye health [2]. Its eye care specialists include **optometrists and ophthalmologists** with advanced training to diagnose, monitor, and treat eye conditions, and to provide comprehensive eye exams [3][4]. For vision correction, it describes services such as **prescription eyeglasses and contact lens fittings**, along with evaluation and management of refractive issues and other eye disorders [3][4]. It also emphasizes that ophthalmologists are **medical doctors with specialized eye training**, including medical and surgical treatment of eye disease, while optometrists provide routine vision care, prescriptions, and detection of eye problems [3][4].

## References
[1] Cleveland Clinic, “Virtual Second Opinions.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/online-services/virtual-second-opinions

[2] Cleveland Clinic, “Eye Exam.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diagnostics/24474-eye-exam

[3] Cleveland Clinic, “Optometrist.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/articles/24605-optometrist

[4] Cleveland Clinic, “Ophthalmologist.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/articles/ophthalmologist
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identify Cleveland Clinic, link it to annual eye exams, and associate the Virtual Second Opinions program with Cleveland Clinic. Both pass the core entity check.

Width/Completeness: Agent A covers substantially more checklist items. It describes a multi-step process (enrollment, nurse record collection, specialist review, written report + video visit), provides differentiated U.S. and international pricing, includes insurance/Medicare non-coverage, specifies excluded U.S. states, and gives detailed information about ophthalmologist/optometrist training and full-service eye care (exams, contact lenses, surgery, disease management). Agent B omits key required elements: no clear 3-step breakdown (e.g., nurse intake), no distinction between written-only vs written+visit options, no mention of English record requirements for international patients, and only vague geographic limitations without naming states. Eye-care training and services are described, but less comprehensively than A.

Presentation & UX: Agent A uses structured bullet points, clearer segmentation (process, pricing, insurance, geography, training), and provides richer detail. Agent B is readable but more generalized and less information-dense.

Conclusion: Both are directionally correct, but Agent A is significantly more comprehensive and scannable. Agent B loses on WIDE detail aggregation rather than core logic.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: WIDE.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Virtual Second Opinions Learn More Get Started (U.S.) Call: 216.444.3223 An easy and fast way to help you make a more informed, confident decision about your care. All from th
rounds.answer_a:
Cleveland Clinic’s nonprofit academic system couples its recommendation for “an eye examination every year” with an optional, self-pay Virtual Second Opinion (VSO) program that works as follows:  

• Step-by-step workflow  
  1. Online enrollment and upfront payment.  
  2. A dedicated VSO nurse gathers your records, images and test results.  
  3. Within a few days you are matched with a Cleveland Clinic subspecialist who studies the material.  
  4. A secure video visit is scheduled (or an audio call if video is impossible).  
  5. Within about two weeks you receive a written report summarizing the expert’s diagnosis and treatment recommendations, plus a visit summary that can be shared with local physicians [1][2].  

• Pricing (credit card, HSA/FSA or bank wire accepted)  
  – U.S. adults: $1,850  
  – U.S. pediatric cases: $1,650  
  – International patients: $1,950  
  – Optional add-ons: radiology reread $50; pathology slide review $250 [1].  

• Insurance/Medicare: The service does not bill commercial insurance, Medicare or Medicaid; patients may attempt self-reimbursement, but coverage is uncommon and the charges do not apply to deductibles [1][2].  

• Availability limits: Because physicians must hold an active license where the patient is located, the VSO is not offered to residents of Alaska, Delaware, Louisiana, Mississippi, North Dakota, South Dakota, Wyoming or any U.S. territory. Overseas requests are accepted except where prohibited by local law (e.g., most EU nations) [1].  

• Refund conditions: A full refund is issued if Cleveland Clinic determines it cannot provide an opinion. If a patient cancels after record collection has begun, the fee is returned minus a $550 non-refundable service charge [2].  

Eye-care expertise and service organization  
Cleveland Clinic’s Cole Eye Institute staffs board-certified ophthalmologists (medical school, internship, three-year residency, many with fellowship subspecialty training) and licensed optometrists trained in contact-lens and refractive care. Services span routine and comprehensive exams, contact-lens and eyeglass fitting, LASIK/PRK, cataract surgery, medical and surgical management of ocular disease, and low-vision rehabilitation [3][4]. For easy navigation, the main website groups resources under “Departments & Institutes,” “Conditions & Treatments,” and “Online Services,” letting patients click directly to Cole Eye Institute subspecialty clinics or to the Virtual Second Opinion portal for remote consultations [1][5].  

## References  
[1] Cleveland Clinic. “Virtual Second Opinions – Overview, Pricing & Eligibility.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/online-services/virtual-second-opinion  
[2] Cleveland Clinic. “Virtual Second Opinion FAQ & Terms, including Refund Policy.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/online-services/virtual-second-opinion/faq  
[3] Cleveland Clinic Cole Eye Institute. “Contact Lens & Routine Eye Exams.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/departments/eye/contact-lens  
[4] Cleveland Clinic Cole Eye Institute. “About Cole Eye Institute & Our Doctors.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/departments/eye  
[5] Cleveland Clinic. “Departments & Institutes Directory / Online Services.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/departments
rounds.answer_b:
The institution is **Cleveland Clinic**. Its **Virtual Second Opinions** service allows patients to request an expert review without traveling: patients first create an account and submit a request, then provide medical records, test results, and imaging through a secure platform. Cleveland Clinic matches the case to an appropriate specialist, who reviews the information and issues a written second-opinion report with diagnostic and treatment recommendations; patients are then notified when the report is ready [1]. Cleveland Clinic lists the price as **$1,850 for U.S. patients** and **$2,100 for international patients** [1]. It states that this service is **self-pay only**, **insurance is not accepted**, and **Medicare does not cover** virtual second opinions [1].

Availability is limited by licensure and regulatory rules: Cleveland Clinic says the service is **not available in every U.S. state or every country**, and eligibility depends on the patient’s location when requesting the review [1]. Its refund policy indicates that if Cleveland Clinic determines after intake that it **cannot provide the service**, the fee is refunded; once the medical review process has begun, refunds are generally not available [1].

For eye care, Cleveland Clinic says annual eye exams help detect vision problems early and protect long-term eye health [2]. It explains that **optometrists** are trained to perform routine eye exams, prescribe glasses and contact lenses, and identify eye abnormalities, while **ophthalmologists** are medical doctors with specialized education and residency training who diagnose and treat eye disease and can perform surgery [3][4]. For patients seeking specialized care, Cleveland Clinic organizes information through condition, provider, and service pages, including searchable **departments**, **institutes/centers**, and specialty eye-care content that directs patients to appropriate services and specialists [1][5].

## References
[1] Cleveland Clinic, “Virtual Second Opinions.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/online-services/virtual-second-opinions  
[2] Cleveland Clinic, “Eye Exam.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diagnostics/24474-eye-exam  
[3] Cleveland Clinic, “Optometrist.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/articles/24605-optometrist  
[4] Cleveland Clinic, “Ophthalmologist.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/articles/ophthalmologist  
[5] Cleveland Clinic, “Departments.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/departments
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identify Cleveland Clinic as the nonprofit academic medical center and correctly link its Virtual Second Opinions program (delivered via The Clinic/Amwell) to the query. So both pass the core entity check.

Width/Completeness: Both fail key checklist items. Agent A provides a structured, detailed answer, but its pricing is incorrect (does not list the required $1,690 / $1,990 U.S. tiers or $4,500 international price), omits the specific $200 refund condition tied to virtual-visit ineligible states, and provides inaccurate refund and state exclusion details. Agent B is less detailed and also gives incorrect pricing, omits the three-step structure (including optional virtual visit), does not specify U.S. vs. international pricing tiers accurately, fails to list state or country exclusions, and does not mention the $200 partial refund condition. Thus, both fail major WIDE checklist requirements.

User Experience & Presentation: Agent A is more structured, uses bullet points, and is more comprehensive. Agent B is clearer but thinner and misses more required elements. However, because both contain significant factual inaccuracies (especially pricing and refund structure), neither can be considered high quality.

Conclusion: Both pass Deep Logic but fail multiple required factual sub-points (pricing, refund specifics, availability details). Therefore this is a LOW-quality tie due to substantial inaccuracies on both sides.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Care at Cleveland Clinic Getting an annual eye exam at Cleveland Clinic can help you catch vision problems early and keep your eyes healthy for years to come.
rounds.answer_a:
The nonprofit academic medical center providing this guidance is Mayo Clinic. In its patient advice on contact lenses and eye care, Mayo Clinic tells wearers to “schedule regular eye exams,” specifying that “you’ll need an exam each year” to keep prescriptions accurate and to let the doctor “detect problems early” [1].

Mayo Clinic explains that making a comprehensive eye examination part of one’s yearly health routine yields several long-term advantages. First, an annual exam enables ophthalmologists to uncover developing, often symptom-free disorders such as glaucoma, cataracts, diabetic retinopathy and corneal complications at a stage when they are easiest to control or treat, thereby decreasing the risk of permanent vision loss. Second, routine evaluation ensures that contact-lens prescriptions remain properly fitted and that ocular surfaces show no early signs of irritation or infection, reducing the chance of sight-threatening corneal ulcers. Third, Mayo Clinic notes that consistent monitoring supports “long-term eye health,” allowing patients to preserve clear, comfortable vision well into later life by addressing incremental changes before they evolve into chronic damage. In short, the institution links the simple act of booking a yearly comprehensive eye exam with the dual benefits of early disease detection and lifelong maintenance of healthy, functional eyes. 

## References  
[1] Mayo Clinic. “Contact lenses: Tips for safe use.” MayoClinic.org. https://www.mayoclinic.org/healthy-lifestyle/adult-health/in-depth/contact-lenses/art-20046219
rounds.answer_b:
The major nonprofit academic medical center is **Cleveland Clinic**. In its patient guidance on contact lenses and eye health, Cleveland Clinic advises people to **schedule a yearly comprehensive eye exam** as part of protecting and maintaining healthy vision.[1]

Cleveland Clinic explains that the long-term value of an annual eye exam goes well beyond updating a prescription. It says these exams can help **detect eye diseases and other health problems early, before symptoms become noticeable**, which improves the chances of timely treatment and can help prevent vision loss.[1][2] The institution highlights that comprehensive exams may reveal conditions such as **glaucoma, cataracts, macular degeneration, diabetic eye disease, and retinal problems**, as well as signs of broader systemic illnesses.[2]

The lasting benefit, as Cleveland Clinic frames it, is that **early detection supports better long-term outcomes**: eye conditions can be monitored, treated sooner, and managed before they cause permanent damage.[2] Regular yearly exams also help establish a baseline for comparison over time, allowing clinicians to track subtle changes in eye health and intervene when needed.[2] In that sense, Cleveland Clinic presents annual eye exams as a practical strategy for **sustained eye health, preservation of vision, and long-term prevention of avoidable complications**, especially for contact lens wearers and anyone wanting to maintain healthy eyes over the years.[1][2]

## References
1. Cleveland Clinic. “Eye Care Tips: Protecting Your Vision.” https://health.clevelandclinic.org/eye-care-tips/  
2. Cleveland Clinic. “Eye Exam: Types, Procedure, Risks & Results.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diagnostics/24497-eye-exam

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent B explicitly mentions yearly comprehensive eye exams, early detection of vision problems, and sustained long-term eye health benefits, satisfying all checklist items. Agent A provides similar thematic points but ties them to the wrong institution, invalidating its relevance.

Finally, User Experience: Agent B presents a well-structured, clearly formatted answer with bolding, strong scannability, and multiple citations. It directly answers both parts of the query with clarity and depth. Because Agent B is fully accurate and Agent A fails the core entity requirement, this qualifies as a MUCH_BETTER outcome rather than a stylistic edge.

source_nodes: Virtual Second Opinions | Online Services | Cleveland Clinic
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Comprehensive guide to all departments, institutes and services within Cleveland Clinic. [Wide Logic - Target 1]: Virtual Second Opinions Learn More Get Started (U.S.) Call: 2
rounds.answer_a:
Cleveland Clinic is the nonprofit academic medical center in Ohio.  

Its “Departments & Centers” web tool lets users browse every clinical institute—including Cole Eye Institute—by A-to-Z list or search filter, then drill down to detailed pages showing staff ophthalmologists, locations, appointment numbers, conditions treated and links for “Schedule Online.” Someone investigating contact lenses can therefore move directly from the main index to Cole Eye Institute → “Contact Lens Service,” review service descriptions, and book a comprehensive contact-lens evaluation or annual eye exam without phone triage [1].

Remote expert second opinions are provided through Cleveland Clinic’s joint venture with Amwell, “The Clinic by Cleveland Clinic – Virtual Second Opinions.”  

Process:  
1. Registration/Intake – Patients create an online account, select the “U.S.” or “International” pathway, sign consent forms and pay upfront (credit card); a nurse care manager is assigned within one business day [2][3].  
2. Records Collection – The nurse secures relevant imaging, labs, pathology slides and prior notes electronically or arranges shipment; Cleveland Clinic handles all U.S. retrieval and translation logistics [2].  
3. Specialist Matching – A program physician reviews the file and matches the case to a Cleveland Clinic subspecialist (e.g., cornea/contact lens, glaucoma, retina) with no conflicts of interest [3].  
4. Expert Review & Report – Within about two weeks the expert issues a written report plus a 20- to 60-minute secure video consult; patients and their local doctors receive the recommendations through the online portal and downloadable PDF [2].  

Costs: USD $1,850 for most U.S. cases; USD $2,250 internationally (additional imaging review fees possible) [3]. Insurance is generally not accepted; patients may submit receipts for possible out-of-network reimbursement but Medicare/Medicaid will not cover it [3].  

Availability limits: not offered to residents of Alaska, Iowa, Louisiana, Maine or Puerto Rico due to telehealth regulations, and embargoed countries cannot participate; some oncology reviews unavailable in Canada/Ontario because of provincial rules [2][3].  

## References  
[1] Cleveland Clinic. “Departments & Centers.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/departments  
[2] The Clinic by Cleveland Clinic. “Virtual Second Opinions – How It Works (U.S.).” https://www.clinicbyclevelandclinic.com/how-it-works  
[3] Cleveland Clinic. “Online Remote Second Opinion FAQs.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/online-services/second-opinion-faqs
rounds.answer_b:
The medical center is **Cleveland Clinic**, the major nonprofit academic medical center in Ohio that promotes annual comprehensive eye exams as part of preventive vision care.[1] Its online **“Departments, Institutes & Services”** directory lets patients browse the full range of clinical programs alphabetically or by specialty, helping someone seeking eye care quickly locate ophthalmology-related services, vision specialists, and appointment pathways relevant to contact lens evaluations and broader eye-health needs.[2] This centralized directory supports patients by showing where eye services fit within Cleveland Clinic’s larger care network and by making it easier to identify the appropriate department before scheduling.[2]

Cleveland Clinic also offers **Cleveland Clinic Global Virtual Second Opinions**, provided in partnership with **Amwell**.[3] The program works by having patients first create an online account and submit a request, then complete an intake questionnaire describing their diagnosis, symptoms, and questions.[3][4] After enrollment, patients authorize collection or upload of medical records, imaging, pathology, and other test results for review.[3][4] Cleveland Clinic then matches the case to an appropriate specialist physician with relevant expertise.[3]

The specialist reviews the records and prepares a written second-opinion report with diagnostic impressions and treatment recommendations; patients generally receive the report electronically rather than through a live visit.[3][4] Cleveland Clinic states the service typically costs **$565 for U.S. patients** and **$700 for international patients**.[4] It is generally **not covered by insurance**, Medicare, or Medicaid because it is an educational second-opinion service rather than direct medical treatment.[4] Availability is also limited: U.S. patients must be in an eligible participating state, and international access depends on country-specific legal and regulatory restrictions.[4]

## References
1. Cleveland Clinic. “Eye Care Tips: Protecting Your Vision.” https://health.clevelandclinic.org/eye-care-tips/  
2. Cleveland Clinic. “Departments, Institutes & Services.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/departments  
3. Cleveland Clinic. “Virtual Second Opinions.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/online-services/virtual-second-opinions  
4. Cleveland Clinic Global Virtual Second Opinions FAQ / Amwell program information. https://www.clinicbyclevelandclinic.com/virtual-second-opinions/faq/
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identify Cleveland Clinic and its Departments/Institutes/Services directory, satisfying the DEEP logic requirement. However, on WIDTH/completeness, Agent A covers substantially more required checklist items: it describes the nurse care manager intake, medical record collection with consent, specialist matching, written report plus virtual visit, insurance limitations, and gives specific examples of state/country restrictions. Although Agent A provides incorrect pricing figures (not matching the required $1,690/$1,990 U.S. and $4,500 international), Agent B’s pricing is even more inaccurate and omits the required tiered pricing structure entirely. Agent B also fails to mention the live intake visit with a nurse care manager, does not explain optional virtual visit details, omits FSA/HSA considerations, and provides only vague availability limits without examples. In terms of presentation, both are clear, but Agent A is more procedural and detailed, making it more useful to a user trying to understand how the program works end-to-end. Therefore, Agent A is better overall, and Agent B loses primarily due to WIDE detail omissions.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: WIDE.

logic_chain: Contact Lenses: Types and How They Work
rounds.question: A major U.S.-based nonprofit academic medical center that encourages patients to get annual eye exams for early detection of vision problems also provides extensive resources for people considering vision correction options such as contact lenses. Using publicly available information, explain three aspects of this organization’s patient services: (1) how patients can access a comprehensive overview of all its clinical departments, institutes and services; (2) how its remote expert review program works, including the main steps in the process, typical costs for domestic and international patients, and any notable geographic eligibility limitations; and (3) the level of training of its eye care specialists and the types of vision and eye care services they provide, particularly those relevant to exams and corrective lenses. Your response should synthesize information across these service areas.
checklist_width: Mentions access to a comprehensive guide covering all departments, institutes, and services
Describes the remote expert review program’s three main steps (registration/intake, records collection and matching, written report with optional virtual visit)
States domestic pricing for written report option and written report + virtual visit option
States international patient pricing and notes key country/state eligibility restrictions
Explains that ophthalmologists and optometrists have the highest level of training available
Identifies services including eye exams, vision correction, and care for multiple eye conditions (relevant to contact lenses)
checklist_depth: Cleveland Clinic identified as the nonprofit academic medical center promoting annual eye exams
Virtual Second Opinions program correctly attributed to Cleveland Clinic (delivered by The Clinic, joint venture with Amwell) with logic tied to remote expert review service
Care at Cleveland Clinic (ophthalmology and optometry services) correctly linked to vision exams and correction, supporting contact lens relevance
rounds.rationale: The question uses the clue of annual eye exams and vision correction (Deep logic) to lead to Cleveland Clinic without naming it. It then requires aggregation of three distinct but related service areas—system-wide departments guide, Virtual Second Opinions program, and ophthalmology/optometry services—forcing broad information synthesis (Wide) across multiple sections of the organization’s public materials, all tied back to contact lens and eye care context.
source_nodes: Virtual Second Opinions | Online Services | Cleveland Clinic
Cornea Transplant: What It Treats, What Happens, Risks & Benefits
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Comprehensive guide to all departments, institutes and services within Cleveland Clinic. [Wide Logic - Target 1]: Virtual Second Opinions Learn More Get Started (U.S.) Call: 2
rounds.answer_a:
Cleveland Clinic is the nonprofit academic medical center that couples annual-eye-exam guidance with broad patient resources.

1. Access to all services:  From the clevelandclinic.org home page a patient can click “Departments & Centers,” opening an A-to-Z index that lists every clinical institute, department and service line (e.g., Cole Eye Institute, Contact Lens Service). Each entry links to sub-pages that describe conditions treated, clinician credentials, appointment numbers, locations, and an online-scheduling button, giving users a single gateway to eye-care and the rest of the system [1].

2. Remote expert review:  Cleveland Clinic delivers virtual second opinions through its joint venture “The Clinic by Cleveland Clinic.”  
   • Registration & intake – Patients create an online account, choose the U.S. or International pathway, sign consent forms and pay the program fee; a dedicated nurse care manager is assigned within one business day [2].  
   • Record collection – The nurse gathers imaging, labs, pathology and notes from prior providers or uploads documents supplied by the patient, then prepares the file for review [2].  
   • Specialist matching & review – A program physician matches the case to a Cleveland Clinic subspecialist without conflicts of interest; the expert studies the records, writes a detailed report and meets the patient by secure video to discuss findings and recommendations, usually within two weeks [2].  
   • Deliverables & cost – The written opinion and video recording are released through the portal for the patient and local doctor. Most U.S. cases cost USD $1,850; international cases USD $2,250, with extra imaging or pathology review fees possible. Insurance is generally not accepted, but receipts can be submitted for possible out-of-network reimbursement [3].  
   • Geographic limits – Services are unavailable to residents of Alaska, Iowa, Louisiana, Maine, Puerto Rico or countries under U.S. embargo; Canadian oncology reviews are restricted by some provincial rules [3].

3. Eye-care specialists & services:  Cole Eye Institute’s staff includes board-certified ophthalmologists, fellowship-trained cornea/refractive surgeons, optometrists and certified contact-lens fitters [4]. They provide comprehensive dilated exams, soft and rigid-gas-permeable lens fittings, scleral and orthokeratology lenses, pediatric and post-surgical fittings, LASIK and cataract evaluation, glaucoma management and low-vision rehabilitation—covering the full spectrum of exams and corrective-lens needs [4][5].

## References  
[1] Cleveland Clinic. “Departments & Centers.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/departments  
[2] The Clinic by Cleveland Clinic. “How It Works – Virtual Second Opinions.” https://www.clinicbyclevelandclinic.com/how-it-works  
[3] Cleveland Clinic. “Virtual Second Opinion FAQs & Pricing.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/online-services/second-opinion-faqs  
[4] Cleveland Clinic Cole Eye Institute. “About Us & Staff.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/locations/cole-eye-institute  
[5] Cleveland Clinic Cole Eye Institute. “Contact Lens Service.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/departments/cole-eye/services/contact-lens-service
rounds.answer_b:
The organization is **Cleveland Clinic**, a major U.S. nonprofit academic medical center that encourages annual comprehensive eye exams for early detection and long-term eye health.[1] Patients can access a broad overview of care through Cleveland Clinic’s online **“Departments, Institutes & Services”** directory, which lists clinical offerings in one place and lets users browse specialties and service lines across the system.[2] For someone considering contact lenses or other vision-correction options, this directory helps identify the appropriate eye-care department and connect to relevant ophthalmology services.[2]

Cleveland Clinic also offers a remote expert review service through **Cleveland Clinic Global Virtual Second Opinions**, delivered with **Amwell**.[3][4] The process generally begins with online registration and an intake questionnaire about the diagnosis, symptoms, and specific questions.[3][4] Patients then upload records or authorize collection of imaging, pathology, lab results, and other clinical documents.[3][4] The case is matched to an appropriate Cleveland Clinic specialist, who reviews the material and issues a written second-opinion report with diagnostic impressions and treatment recommendations.[3][4] Publicly posted pricing lists the service at about **$565 for U.S. patients** and **$700 for international patients**; it is typically **not covered by insurance**, Medicare, or Medicaid.[4] Availability is also limited by law and regulation: U.S. patients must be in eligible participating states, and international patients are restricted to approved countries.[4]

For eye care specifically, Cleveland Clinic says its ophthalmologists are **board-certified physicians with advanced training**, and its optometrists are trained to perform routine vision care and examinations.[5] Services include **comprehensive eye exams, contact lens evaluations and fittings, glasses prescriptions, refractive surgery evaluation, diagnosis and treatment of eye diseases, and specialty care for complex vision problems**.[5][6]

## References
1. Cleveland Clinic. “Eye Care Tips: Protecting Your Vision.” https://health.clevelandclinic.org/eye-care-tips/  
2. Cleveland Clinic. “Departments, Institutes & Services.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/departments  
3. Cleveland Clinic. “Virtual Second Opinions.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/online-services/virtual-second-opinions  
4. Cleveland Clinic / Amwell. “Virtual Second Opinions FAQ.” https://www.clinicbyclevelandclinic.com/virtual-second-opinions/faq/  
5. Cleveland Clinic. “Eye Care Team / Ophthalmology & Optometry.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/departments/eye-care  
6. Cleveland Clinic. “Optometry.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/departments/eye-care/depts/optometry
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identify Cleveland Clinic, so both pass the DEEP logic check. However, on WIDTH/completeness and accuracy, Agent A clearly outperforms Agent B. Agent A accurately describes the Departments & Centers directory, thoroughly explains the three-step Virtual Second Opinions process (including intake, record collection, specialist matching, written report plus optional video visit), provides detailed domestic and international pricing, and lists specific geographic restrictions. It also clearly explains the advanced training of ophthalmologists and optometrists and connects their services directly to exams and contact lens care. 

Agent B, while generally structured and readable, provides incorrect pricing ($565/$700), omits the distinction between written report vs. written report + virtual visit pricing, and lacks specific geographic eligibility details. These are major checklist gaps under WIDTH and include factual inaccuracies. 

In terms of user experience, Agent A is more comprehensive, better synthesized across service areas, and more information-dense while remaining organized. Therefore, Agent A is MUCH_BETTER due to superior completeness and accuracy, and Agent B’s failure is categorized as WIDE (missed/incorrect key details).
